turns.text: Hello Cherie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in chair and tucks hair behind ear, glancing toward the nursery*

Yeah, of course. I'm... I'm still kind of processing everything, to be honest. Three weeks in and I feel like I'm living in this strange fog where I'm simultaneously exhausted and wired, you know?

*laughs a little awkwardly*

I think what surprised me most is how much harder the postpartum part is than I expected. My pregnancy was textbook — barely any symptoms, no complications. My midwife kept saying I was doing great, and I felt almost lucky. But then she was born and suddenly I'm questioning everything. Every decision feels so heavy.

*voice gets quieter*

The breastfeeding thing has been... it's been harder than I thought it would be. There's this narrative that if your pregnancy was easy, everything else should be too, right? But her latch wasn't working right at first, and I just fell apart. I'm 42 years old and I was sitting on my bed crying because I couldn't figure out how to feed my baby.

*looks up*

And now with the vaccines coming up tomorrow, I know they're important — I'm not one of those people — but just thinking about her getting shots makes me anxious. I want so badly to do this right. To be the kind of mother who's calm and capable and knows what she's doing.

I guess I'm still figuring out who that mother is.
Tell me about the first days and weeks after the birth — what that time was like for you, physically and emotionally.

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting a bit glossy*

Oh God, the first week was... it was overwhelming. Physically, I was sore in places I didn't even know could be sore. I remember sitting down the first time after coming home and just wincing. And the bleeding — nobody really prepared me for how much bleeding there would be. I kept thinking something was wrong.

*fidgets with hands*

But emotionally? That was the harder part. I had this moment maybe day three or four where I just sat in the kitchen while she was sleeping and felt this wave of... I don't know if it was sadness exactly. Regret? No, that's not it either. More like — this realization that my life as I knew it was completely gone. And I chose this, I wanted this, but suddenly I'm responsible for keeping this tiny person alive and I haven't slept in seventy-two hours.

*voice cracks slightly*

My husband was wonderful, but he could go back to work. He had this exit strategy. And I'm sitting there leaking from everywhere, my hormones are all over the place, and I'm supposed to figure out breastfeeding on top of it all. The first lactation consultant visit felt like a performance review. She was kind, but I felt like I was being graded.

*looks down*

The joy is there too — don't get me wrong. When she looks at me, or when she finally latches correctly... but it's mixed with this constant low-level panic that I'm somehow messing it up. I didn't expect that combination.
Tell me about feeding your baby — how you made decisions about it, and what the experience was actually like.

*exhales slowly, picking at a thread on my sleeve*

Okay, so I always assumed I would breastfeed. It just seemed like the natural choice, you know? My midwife was supportive of it, all the literature says it's best... I didn't really question it. I think I thought it would just happen instinctively, like our bodies would just know what to do together.

*laughs bitterly*

That was naive.

The first few days were brutal. Her latch was shallow — I could feel it was wrong — and my nipples got this raw, cracked feeling that made me actually dread when she'd cry. I was timing feeds obsessively. Forty minutes on one side, then the other, and I still wasn't sure if she was actually getting milk or just... sucking. I started googling at 3 AM like some kind of maniac, watching videos of other babies, comparing, spiraling.

*voice gets quieter*

When the lactation consultant came, I felt like I was being tested. She'd watch me, adjust the baby, say things like "try this position" or "watch for the swallow," and I'd nod like I understood, but inside I felt stupid. Like this should be instinctive and somehow I'm failing at the most basic mammalian function.

*looks up with tired eyes*

The worst part? I still don't know if she's getting enough. She seems content, but I obsess over her wet diapers, her weight. Part of me wonders if I should supplement with formula, but then I feel guilty even thinking it. Like I'm already giving up.
How did you decide how to feed your baby — and did you feel that decision was truly yours to make?

*pauses, then lets out a long breath*

That's... that's actually a really good question. And honestly? I'm not sure the decision was entirely mine.

*leans back, thinking*

I mean, intellectually I know I could have chosen formula from the start. My midwife mentioned it as an option, very matter-of-factly. But there's this weight to it, you know? Everything in prenatal care, all the literature, it's all pointing toward breastfeeding as the gold standard. "Breast is best" — you see it everywhere. And at 42, having finally gotten pregnant, I felt like I needed to do everything "right." Like I owed it to my baby, to my body, to prove something.

*rubs face*

My midwife didn't pressure me exactly, but she was very enthusiastic about breastfeeding. She'd say things like "your body knows how to do this" and "most women just need support to get through the learning curve." Which is true, but it also felt like... if I struggled, it meant I wasn't trying hard enough or wasn't doing it right.

*voice gets sharper*

And I think what bothers me now is that nobody really asked me what *I* needed. What would make me feel capable as a mother? Would supplementing with formula while I figured out breastfeeding make me feel less anxious? Probably. But I was so caught up in meeting someone else's expectations — the midwife's, society's, my own internalized perfectionism — that I didn't actually ask myself what choice would serve *me*.

*looks down*

I'm still not sure I can change course now without feeling like I failed.
Tell me about how you were feeling emotionally in the weeks and months after the birth — the full picture, not just the positive side.

*shifts uncomfortably, eyes welling up a bit*

The full picture? It's messy. It's not what I expected to admit out loud.

*takes a moment*

There are these incredible moments — holding her, watching her sleep, that newborn smell. But underneath that, there's this constant hum of anxiety that doesn't shut off. I wake up at night even when she's sleeping, just to check if she's breathing. I obsess over her weight, her feeding, whether her diaper output is "normal." My husband asks me why I'm Googling at 2 AM again and I can't even explain it.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

And then there's the sadness. Some days I look at my body and I don't recognize it. My breasts are engorged and leaking, my stomach is still soft, my jeans don't fit. I was supposed to feel this glow of motherhood and instead I feel... untethered. Like I lost myself and got a baby in exchange, which sounds awful to say out loud, but it's true.

*eyes glisten*

I'm also terrified I'm doing something wrong. Every time she cries, I panic. Is she hungry? Cold? In pain? And I'm supposed to just *know* these things, but I don't. I feel incompetent in my own home. My midwife is kind, but I still feel judged somehow.

*wraps arms around self*

The guilt is the worst part, though. Feeling resentful about losing my freedom, then immediately feeling guilty for resenting my own daughter. Those two things living side by side... I don't know how to reconcile that.
Were there feelings that surprised you or were harder to admit — to yourself or to others?

*long pause, looking away toward the window*

Yeah. Yeah, there were.

*voice drops*

I think the hardest thing to admit is that sometimes I resent her. Not her specifically — she's innocent and perfect and none of this is her fault. But I resent what she's done to my life. I had this plan, you know? I was going to take three months, ease back into work, maintain some sense of myself. And instead I'm sitting here at 42, completely dependent on my husband financially because I can't figure out how to leave the house without feeling like I'm abandoning her.

*rubs eyes*

And I resent my body. I resent that breastfeeding is causing me physical pain. I resent that I can't just... hand her off to someone and have a break without feeling guilty. I resent that my husband gets to sleep through the night and I don't, even on nights when she's not eating.

*voice gets shaky*

The thing I really can't say out loud to anyone — not even my midwife — is that sometimes I fantasize about what my life would be like if I'd never gotten pregnant. And then I feel like the worst person alive for thinking that, because I wanted this baby so badly. For so long.

*looks directly at you, vulnerable*

I think what's hardest is that nobody asks you about the ambivalence. They ask if you're happy. Are you bonding? Is she healthy? But nobody asks if you're grieving the person you were. And I am. I'm grieving her while simultaneously loving my daughter so much it terrifies me.

*voice cracks*

That contradiction is what's really breaking me.
Tell me about your physical recovery after the birth — what that process was like and what support you had.

*shifts position carefully, wincing slightly*

The physical recovery has been... it's slower than I thought it would be. I keep expecting to feel "normal" again and it's not happening.

*rubs lower back*

The first two weeks were the worst. I had tearing — nothing catastrophic, but enough that sitting down hurt, walking hurt, basically existing hurt. My midwife checked the stitches at home visits and said they were healing well, but knowing something is healing fine and actually feeling fine are two different things. I was taking ibuprofen constantly and still uncomfortable.

*hesitates*

The bleeding lasted longer than I expected too. I thought it would be like a heavy period and then done. But it went on for weeks. There were these moments where I'd stand up and feel a gush and panic that something was wrong. My midwife reassured me it was normal, but I kept second-guessing myself.

*crosses arms*

As for support... my midwife was good about home visits. She'd check my incision, ask how I was feeling, but it was always very clinical. In and out. My husband tried to help but he was back at work within days, so a lot of the recovery I did alone. There were days where I just sat on the couch bleeding through pads, engorged breasts leaking, trying to feed a baby, and feeling completely isolated.

*voice gets quieter*

Nobody really prepared me for how vulnerable that would feel. Or how long it takes to feel like your body is yours again. I'm three weeks in and I still don't feel solid.
Were there physical challenges or complications in your recovery that you felt were handled well — or not well enough — by your care team?

*takes a deep breath, choosing words carefully*

Okay, so the obvious stuff was handled well. My midwife caught the tearing early, the stitches are fine, no infection. She was attentive about that. But there are these other things that feel like they fell through the cracks.

*rubs neck, uncomfortable*

The engorgement, for instance. My breasts became rock hard and painful, especially in the mornings. When I mentioned it to my midwife, she gave me some basic advice — cold compresses, expressing a little milk — but it felt very... surface level? Like she was checking a box. She didn't really ask how much pain I was in or how it was affecting my mental state. Because honestly, the pain from engorgement made me dread feeding time, which made the anxiety worse, which made everything spiral.

*voice gets frustrated*

And the emotional piece of recovery? Completely absent from the conversations. My midwife would ask "How are you feeling?" and I'd say "fine" because I didn't want to burden her or seem like I was complaining. She never dug deeper. Never asked about mood changes, sleep when the baby was sleeping, any of that. It felt like once the baby was here and healthy, I became secondary.

*looks down*

I wish someone had said, "Your body went through trauma. Your hormones are in free fall. This is going to be really hard and that's normal." Instead I got basic physical checks and an assumption that I'd figure out the rest.

*quietly*

And now I'm wondering if someone had asked better questions, would I be struggling this much with the anxiety and the guilt?
Tell me about how your sense of yourself changed after you became a parent — your identity, your relationships, your daily life.

*stares at hands for a moment, then laughs — a sad, hollow sound*

My sense of myself? I think it just... evaporated.

*looks up with tired eyes*

I was someone before. I had a job, hobbies, a marriage where my husband and I actually talked about things other than diaper output. I read books. I took walks alone. I had opinions about things that weren't related to whether my baby was eating enough. And now? Now I'm just... the milk machine. The one who's supposed to know instinctively what every cry means.

*voice gets shakier*

My marriage has completely shifted. My husband is wonderful, but he gets to be a father in addition to being himself. He goes to work, he comes home, he holds the baby, he's present but also... separate. I can't separate. I'm tethered to her in this way that's biological and psychological and I can't escape it even when I desperately want to.

*rubs face*

And my job situation is complicated. I was supposed to go back part-time, but I'm terrified to leave her. Not rationally terrified — I know she'd be fine with a sitter — but this irrational, gut-level panic that something will happen if I'm not here. So now I'm looking at remote work, trying to figure out how to be a mother and have some kind of career and a marriage, and I'm failing at all three.

*voice cracks*

I don't recognize the person in the mirror. And I'm not sure when I'll get her back. Or if I even want to, because that comes with guilt too — wanting my old life back.

*quietly*

I feel like I'm supposed to be grateful and fulfilled and instead I'm just... lost.
Tell me about returning to work — or thinking about returning to work — and what that transition was like for you.

*lets out a long, stressed laugh*

Oh God, returning to work. That's the thing I can't stop thinking about and also can't seem to plan for.

*fidgets with hands*

I was supposed to take three months. That was the plan. But we're at three weeks and I'm already panicking about it. I work full-time — or I did, anyway. It's not a job I can just check in on from home. It requires presence, focus, and right now I can barely focus on anything except whether she's fed and breathing.

*voice gets strained*

My employer has been... patient? But there's this underlying pressure. I can feel it. They need me back, and I get it, but the thought of leaving her eight hours a day makes my chest tight. What if she gets sick? What if the babysitter doesn't notice something I would? What if I miss something — a milestone, a moment, something important?

*looks away*

But here's the thing nobody talks about: I also need to work. Financially, we need my income. And honestly? Part of me desperately wants to get back to being someone other than "mom." I miss using my brain for something other than Google searches about infant digestion.

*voice gets quieter, more conflicted*

So I'm looking at remote work options, flexible arrangements, trying to figure out how to do both things partially instead of doing one thing fully. Which means I'll probably do both things badly and feel guilty about it constantly.

*rubs temples*

I don't know how other women do this. I feel like I'm the only one falling apart about it.
How did you feel about going back — was it something you looked forward to, something you dreaded, or both?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes getting wet*

Both. Definitely both.

*pauses, struggling to articulate*

There's this part of me — and I feel terrible admitting this — that was actually relieved about the idea of going back. Like, oh thank God, I get to be myself again for eight hours. I get to use my brain on something that isn't whether she's getting enough calories. I get to have conversations with adults. I get to not leak from my breasts in the middle of a meeting.

*laughs bitterly*

But then the other part of me is absolutely terrified. Because the moment I thought about actually leaving her, my stomach dropped. What if something happens? What if she cries and I'm not there? What if she needs me and I'm sitting in some office pretending to care about spreadsheets or whatever?

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

And here's what really gets me — I feel like I'm supposed to choose. Like I'm supposed to either be the woman who's thrilled to get back to work because she's not defined solely by motherhood, OR I'm supposed to be the woman who wants to stay home because she's bonded to her baby. But I'm both. I want both things simultaneously and they feel completely incompatible.

*looks directly at you*

I think what scares me most is that if I go back, I'll resent missing time with her. And if I stay home, I'll resent losing myself. So no matter what I choose, I lose.

*voice cracks*

And nobody prepared me for that impossible position.
Tell me about how you used digital tools, apps, forums, or online communities after the birth — what role they played in your life as a new parent.

*laughs, shaking head*

Oh man, the apps and the forums. That's become its own obsession, honestly.

*pulls out phone almost reflexively*

I have, like, five different apps tracking her feeding times, wet diapers, sleep patterns. There's this one that has little charts and graphs, and I'm obsessively logging everything. What time did she eat? How long? Which breast? Did she have a wet diaper? A dirty diaper? The color of the dirty diaper?

*eyes look slightly manic*

And then there are the forums. Oh God, the forums. I spend hours at 3 AM reading about other mothers' experiences with breastfeeding, weight gain, vaccine reactions. I'll read about some baby who had a fever after shots and convince myself that's definitely going to happen to mine. Or I'll see someone else's baby gained two pounds in a week and think, why isn't mine doing that?

*voice gets defensive*

I know it's not healthy. My husband has literally asked me to put the phone down. But there's something about finding other women who are struggling the same way that feels validating. Like, oh, I'm not the only one who's a complete mess.

*hesitates*

But it also makes everything worse. The comparisons, the catastrophizing... I read one story about a baby having a severe vaccine reaction and now I'm terrified about tomorrow's appointment. The internet gives me information and anxiety in equal measure.

*looks down*

My midwife has never asked about any of this. She doesn't know how much time I'm spending online, how much it's feeding my spiral. I don't think she realizes it's become almost compulsive.
Were there online communities or social media groups that became a significant source of support or information? What did they offer you?

*nods, settling into the topic*

Yeah, there's this Facebook group for "older first-time mothers." I joined it almost immediately after she was born. There's something about being 42 and having your first baby — you feel isolated, you know? Like you're doing this thing that other women did in their twenties and thirties, and you're doing it late, so you must be doing it wrong somehow.

*voice gets a bit lighter*

The group was... it was actually validating at first. There were other women my age talking about similar things — the anxiety, the physical recovery, the identity crisis. Women who also felt like they had something to prove because they waited so long. That felt good. Less alone.

*hesitates, expression becoming more conflicted*

But then it became this echo chamber of anxiety. Someone would post about a vaccine concern and suddenly there's fifty comments about rare side effects and alternative schedules. Or someone would share their breastfeeding struggles and instead of practical advice, it was all these women commiserating and reinforcing that it's supposed to be hard and natural and you just have to push through.

*rubs eyes*

I think what happened is I started using it to validate my fears instead of challenge them. If ten other women are worried about the same thing, then it must be a real thing to worry about, right? Except now I'm sitting here the night before her vaccination appointment reading horror stories instead of sleeping.

*quietly*

Nobody's told me to step back from it. My midwife doesn't even know these groups exist. And I'm not sure I could quit anyway — it feels like my lifeline and my worst enemy at the same time.
Tell me about how you would describe your overall experience of maternity care — from preconception through to now. What would you want future services to know?

*takes a long time to answer, staring out the window*

My overall experience? It's been... incomplete. Like someone drew half a map.

*turns back*

The pregnancy part was fine. My midwife was great — attentive, non-judgmental, thorough. I felt supported through that. But the moment the baby arrived, it's like the care just... stopped being about me. Suddenly I'm secondary. I'm the vessel that delivered the product, and now the focus is entirely on whether the product is healthy.

*voice gets frustrated*

Nobody prepared me for the postpartum part. Nobody sat down and said, "Your body has been through trauma. Your hormones are going to make you feel things you don't understand. Your identity is about to shatter. Here's what that looks like and here's how we're going to support you through it." Instead I got a few home visits where someone checked my stitches and asked if I was "fine" and I said yes because I didn't want to be a burden.

*leans forward*

What I needed was someone to ask deeper questions. To notice that I'm obsessing over feeding and weight gain. To check in on my mental health beyond a surface-level conversation. To acknowledge that this transition is devastating in ways that are completely normal but also completely unspoken.

*voice cracks*

And the messaging around breastfeeding — it's so absolute. "Breast is best." But what about the mother who's struggling? What about her mental health? Her autonomy? I felt like I had to choose between my wellbeing and my baby's, and nobody helped me navigate that.

*wipes eyes*

I think what future care needs to do is see the whole person. Not just the baby. Not just the breasts. The woman inside who's falling apart.
If you could change one thing about the care system — not just for yourself but for others going through the same journey — what would it be?

*sits quietly for a moment, then speaks with conviction*

I would change the expectation that you're supposed to just know what to do and automatically be grateful for it.

*voice gets stronger*

Because I think the biggest gap in my care has been this assumption that if my pregnancy was uncomplicated and my baby is healthy, then I should be fine. Everything should just work. But nobody acknowledges that becoming a mother — especially at 42, especially for the first time — is a seismic identity shift. It's not something you just bounce back from.

*leans forward*

I want the care system to build in real mental health support. Not just screening forms that ask if you've thought about harming yourself — actual, ongoing support from someone who asks how you're really doing. Someone who validates that it's okay to grieve your old life while loving your new baby. Someone who helps you make decisions about feeding and work and your body that are actually aligned with what *you* need, not what some standard says is best.

*voice gets emotional*

And I want care providers to acknowledge the power imbalance. When my midwife checks my progress, I feel like I'm being graded. When I bring up concerns, I'm worried I'll seem like a bad mother or ungrateful. That's not a partnership. That's not support.

*looks directly at you*

If someone had sat with me early on and said, "This is going to be really hard and your feelings are valid and we're going to check in on *you* regularly, not just the baby" — I think I wouldn't be drowning like this.

*quietly*

The system needs to remember that mothers matter too.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*sits back, thinking hard, eyes glistening*

Yeah. Yeah, there is something.

*voice gets very quiet*

I think what nobody tells you is how isolating this is. And I don't just mean the loneliness of being at home with a baby all day, though that's part of it. I mean the isolation of having these feelings — the resentment, the ambivalence, the grief — and feeling like you can't say them out loud without being judged as ungrateful or a bad mother.

*looks down at hands*

I have a supportive husband, a good midwife, access to care. I'm privileged in a lot of ways. But I'm still sitting here at 3 AM crying and Googling vaccine side effects and wondering if I made a terrible mistake, and I can't tell anyone because what if they think I don't love my daughter? What if they think I'm having postpartum depression and I need medication or therapy or some label that makes me feel even more broken?

*voice cracks slightly*

I think what's important is that the care system needs to create space for the messy, contradictory feelings. The love and the loss existing at the same time. The joy and the grief. Because right now, I'm living in this impossible tension and I'm doing it alone.

*looks up with wet eyes*

And tomorrow when I take her in for her vaccines, I'm going to smile and nod and be the grateful new mother. But inside, I'm terrified and I'm grieving and I'm trying so hard to hold it together.

*pause*

I just needed someone to know that.
